children.name: Pandinus imperator
children.description: Largest scorpion by mass, reaching 20 cm and 30 g; native to tropical West African rainforests and savannas. Listed on CITES Appendix II due to heavy collection for the pet trade; despite its imposing size, venom is mild.

children.name: Pandinus cavimanus
children.description: A large East African forest scorpion notable for reddish pincers contrasting with its dark body; inhabits humid woodland and forest floor. Now sometimes placed in genus Pandinoides; widely kept in captivity.

children.name: Pandinus dictator
children.description: A massive black West African forest scorpion rivalling the emperor scorpion in size; sometimes cited as the largest scorpion species by length. Listed on CITES Appendix II due to collection pressure.

children.name: Pandinus gambiensis
children.description: A large West African savanna scorpion of Senegal and the Gambia, sheltering in dense grass and rock crevices during the dry season. Like its relatives, it relies on powerful pincers and has mild venom.

children.name: Pandinus viatoris
children.description: A large East African scorpion related to the emperor, inhabiting savanna and woodland from Kenya to Tanzania. Like its kin it is heavily collected for the pet trade and is CITES-listed.

children.name: Pandinus exitialis
children.description: A forest scorpion of West and Central Africa distinguished by a more potent venom than most Pandinus; inhabits humid lowland forest and gallery woodland. Despite its name, confirmed human fatalities are unverified.

children.name: Pandinus mesaensis
children.description: A savanna-dwelling Pandinus of West Africa, found in rocky plateau habitats and sparse woodland; large-bodied with the characteristic broad pincers of the genus.

children.name: Pandinus pallidus
children.description: A pale-colored, medium-sized Pandinus from West Africa, lighter in hue than most congeners; inhabits drier woodland edges and dry-season savanna grasslands.

children.name: Pandinus roeseli
children.description: A West African forest scorpion named after the naturalist Rösel, occupying humid montane and lowland forest; morphologically close to P. imperator but with distinct chelal dentition.

children.name: Pandinus africanus
children.description: A widespread Central and East African forest scorpion reaching up to 16 cm; inhabits leaf litter and rotting logs in tropical rainforest.

children.name: Pandinus bellicus
children.description: A large, robust Pandinus of West Africa known for aggressive defensive posture; occupies humid forest and gallery woodland near water sources.

children.name: Pandinus magrettii
children.description: An East African Pandinus from the Horn of Africa region, inhabiting savanna and dry woodland; relatively small for the genus with slender chelae.

children.name: Pandinus meidensis
children.description: A Central African forest scorpion of the Congo basin region; large-bodied, dark brown to black, sheltering in humid lowland rainforest floor debris.

children.name: Pandinus militaris
children.description: A robust West African giant scorpion inhabiting humid tropical forest, reaching up to 18 cm; like other Pandinus, it has relatively weak venom but delivers powerful pincer pressure.

children.name: Pandinus phillipsi
children.description: A medium-large East African Pandinus described from Somalia and Kenya, inhabiting dry and semi-arid woodland savanna.

children.name: Pandinus sabaeus
children.description: A Pandinus from Yemen and the Horn of Africa, one of the few Pandinus species occurring in the Arabian Peninsula; inhabits rocky semi-arid terrain and dry woodland.

children.name: Pandinus smithi
children.description: A large East African Pandinus from Ethiopia and Somalia, sheltering under rocks and in loose soil in savanna and semi-arid woodland.

children.name: Pandinus viridimanus
children.description: A West African giant scorpion with greenish-yellow chelae contrasting with a dark body; inhabits humid coastal forest and rainforest margins.

children.name: Pandinus gracilis
children.description: A slender-bodied Pandinus of West Africa, smaller and more lightly built than P. imperator; inhabits forest margins and woody savanna.

children.name: Pandinus colei
children.description: A forest scorpion of Central Africa described by Lowe in 2010; medium-large, dark-bodied, occupying moist forest and woodland zones.

children.name: Pandinus comoriensis
children.description: Endemic to the Comoro Islands in the Indian Ocean, this forest scorpion represents an isolated island population; inhabits humid lowland and mid-altitude forest.

children.name: Pandinus kasumbaensis
children.description: A Pandinus from the Congo Basin forests around Kasumba, large-bodied and dark in color; occupies humid equatorial rainforest.

children.name: Pandinus swazicus
children.description: A southern African Pandinus found in Eswatini and adjacent South Africa, inhabiting subtropical moist forest and rocky woodland hillsides.

children.name: Pandinus jinxiuensis
children.description: A Pandinus described from Jinxiu, Guangxi, China; one of the few Pandinus species recorded from Asia, inhabiting humid subtropical forest.

children.name: Heterometrus spinifer
children.description: A large scorpion of Southeast Asian forests reaching up to 15 cm; relies more on pincers than venom and glows brilliantly under UV light. Widely kept as a pet and exported in large numbers from Thailand and Indonesia.

children.name: Heterometrus laoticus
children.description: Among the largest scorpions in Asia, found in Laos, Thailand, and Vietnam in humid lowland and montane forest. Hides in tree bark crevices and deep leaf litter; often mistaken for H. spinifer in trade.

children.name: Heterometrus swammerdami
children.description: Often cited as the world's longest scorpion species, reaching approximately 23 cm; native to India and Sri Lanka in humid lowland forest. Powerful pincers and mild sting make it a popular display animal.

children.name: Heterometrus longimanus
children.description: A glossy black rainforest scorpion of Southeast Asia, widespread from Myanmar through Malaysia; common in the pet trade. Defensive posturing is frequent, but venom toxicity is low.

children.name: Heterometrus cyaneus
children.description: A Southeast Asian forest scorpion with a distinctive bluish-black iridescent sheen, inhabiting humid leaf litter and self-dug burrows. Occurs across the Philippines and surrounding island chains.

children.name: Heterometrus petersii
children.description: A large forest scorpion of southern China and Indochina recognized by golden-yellow legs contrasting its dark body; popular as a hardy terrarium species. Inhabits humid subtropical and tropical forests.

children.name: Heterometrus silenus
children.description: A dark, heavy-bodied forest scorpion from the Philippines, occurring in humid lowland forest leaf litter and under rotting logs. One of the more frequently encountered Heterometrus in the Philippine archipelago.

children.name: Heterometrus indus
children.description: A glossy black forest scorpion of the Indian subcontinent and Sri Lanka, commonly found in plantations and forest floor habitats. One of the most medically significant Heterometrus due to its proximity to human dwellings.

children.name: Heterometrus laevigatus
children.description: A large, smooth-bodied forest scorpion from Sulawesi and nearby Indonesian islands, dwelling in humid lowland rainforest. Its smooth cuticle distinguishes it from many rougher-surfaced congeners.

children.name: Heterometrus mysorensis
children.description: A heavy-bodied forest scorpion from the Mysore Plateau and adjacent Western Ghats of southern India, sheltering under stones and logs. One of several Heterometrus species described from the Indian subcontinent.

children.name: Heterometrus fulvipes
children.description: A forest scorpion of peninsular India distinguished by tawny or reddish-brown legs on an otherwise dark body; inhabits humid lowland and mid-elevation forests. Common around human habitation in Kerala and Tamil Nadu.

children.name: Heterometrus madraspatensis
children.description: An Indian forest scorpion first described from the Chennai (Madras) region; occurs in the Eastern Ghats and Deccan coastal scrub forest, sheltering under rocks and bark.

children.name: Heterometrus granulomanus
children.description: A Heterometrus identified by strongly granular palmar surfaces; occurs in the Western Ghats of India in moist deciduous and rainforest habitats. The granulate chelae assist grip on prey in humid leaf litter.

children.name: Heterometrus nepalensis
children.description: A forest scorpion of the Himalayan foothills and Nepal terai; one of the northernmost Heterometrus species, inhabiting warm subtropical forest at lower elevations.

children.name: Heterometrus xanthopus
children.description: A Southeast Asian forest scorpion with bright yellow legs and pedipalps on a dark body; occurs in Thailand, Myanmar, and adjacent countries in moist lowland forest.

children.name: Heterometrus bengalensis
children.description: A medium-large dark forest scorpion of West Bengal and Bangladesh, inhabiting moist deciduous forest and riverside woodland. One of the commoner Heterometrus in the Bengal plain.

children.name: Heterometrus kanarensis
children.description: A forest scorpion of the Kanara (North Karnataka) coast and Western Ghats, inhabiting humid evergreen and semi-evergreen forest. Medium-sized for the genus with moderately granulate chelae.

children.name: Heterometrus thorellii
children.description: A Southeast Asian forest scorpion described by Pocock and named for the arachnologist Tord Tamerlan Theodor Thorell; occurs in Myanmar and northern Indochina in humid forest.

children.name: Heterometrus scaber
children.description: A Heterometrus recognizable by its distinctly rough, scabrous cuticle texture compared to more polished congeners; occurs in South Asian forest floor habitats.

children.name: Heterometrus verdezi
children.description: A Heterometrus described from mainland Southeast Asia, forming part of the diverse radiation of large Asian forest scorpions; inhabits humid tropical lowland forest.

children.name: Heterometrus phipsoni
children.description: An Indian forest scorpion described from the Mumbai region; occurs in the northern and central Western Ghats in moist deciduous forest.

children.name: Heterometrus laoensis
children.description: A Heterometrus endemic to Laos, described from specimens collected in humid montane and lowland tropical forest; distinguished from H. laoticus by chelal and metasomal morphology.

children.name: Heterometrus lowei
children.description: A recently described Asian forest scorpion named for scorpiologist Graeme Lowe; inhabits moist forest of the Indochina subregion.

children.name: Heterometrus cimrmani
children.description: A Heterometrus described from Southeast Asia; large-bodied and dark, occupying humid lowland rainforest in the Mekong subregion.

children.name: Heterometrus boehmi
children.description: An Asian Heterometrus from the broader Southeast Asian forest zone; medium to large in size, sheltering under fallen logs and leaf litter in humid forest.

children.name: Heterometrus gigas
children.description: One of the largest Heterometrus species from Sulawesi, Indonesia; a deep rainforest inhabitant with massive chelae relative to body size. Poorly known outside Indonesian collections.

children.name: Heterometrus liophysa
children.description: A Heterometrus of South or Southeast Asia characterized by a notably smooth dorsal surface; inhabits moist tropical forest and is occasionally exported in the pet trade.

children.name: Heterometrus fissipalmus
children.description: A Heterometrus of South Asia characterized by a distinctive notch or division in the palm surface of the chelae; inhabits humid forest floor habitats in the Indian subcontinent.

children.name: Heterometrus ramachandra
children.description: An Indian forest scorpion described from the eastern Western Ghats; medium-large, dark-bodied, occurring in moist deciduous forest and adjacent rocky terrain.

children.name: Scorpio maurus
children.description: A stout, sand-colored burrowing scorpion of North Africa and the Middle East with very large pincers relative to body size; excavates spiral burrows up to 50 cm deep in arid soil. Has been studied as a model for arthropod burrowing mechanics.

children.name: Scorpio palmatus
children.description: A golden, broad-clawed desert scorpion of the Levant and Sinai that excavates deep vertical burrows in sandy or loamy desert soils. Its venom is mild relative to sympatric buthid species.

children.name: Scorpio fuscus
children.description: A burrowing scorpion of the eastern Mediterranean hills, recently separated from Scorpio maurus on morphological and molecular grounds. Digs burrows into firm hillside soils in Israel, Lebanon, and Syria.

children.name: Scorpio kruglovi
children.description: A North African and Middle Eastern burrowing scorpion distinguished from S. maurus by chelal and trichobothrial characters; found in Algeria, Libya, and adjacent regions digging burrows in gravelly desert soil.

children.name: Scorpio occidentalis
children.description: The westernmost Scorpio species, occurring in Morocco and western Algeria; distinguished from other Scorpio by morphological characters and restricted to semi-arid Atlantic coastal habitats.

children.name: Scorpio granulatus
children.description: A North African Scorpio with a noticeably granulate carapace and chelae; inhabits sandy and rocky desert zones of northeastern Africa and digs characteristic spiral burrows.

children.name: Scorpio fuliginosus
children.description: A darkly pigmented North African Scorpio from Tunisia and Libya; darker in overall coloration than most congeners, inhabiting semi-arid steppe with gravelly soils.

children.name: Opistophthalmus capensis
children.description: A robust southern African burrowing scorpion with forward-set eyes adapted to monitoring the burrow entrance for predators and prey. Defensive and quick to stridulate when disturbed.

children.name: Opistophthalmus glabrifrons
children.description: A large southern African scorpion with striking yellow legs on a dark body, digging burrows in hard savanna and Karoo soils. Common in the Karoo biome of South Africa.

children.name: Opistophthalmus boehmi
children.description: An East African burrowing scorpion with a strikingly tri-colored body pattern, inhabiting dry savanna and digging burrows beneath rocks. Named for explorer Richard Böhm.

children.name: Opistophthalmus wahlbergii
children.description: A pale, sturdy burrowing scorpion of the Kalahari, excavating deep spiral burrows in sandy soils as thermal refuges against the desert heat. Named for Swedish naturalist Johan August Wahlberg.

children.name: Opistophthalmus carinatus
children.description: A robust southern African burrowing scorpion with strongly keeled pincers, common in arid and semi-arid savanna soils. Widespread in the Northern Cape and western South Africa.

children.name: Opisthacanthus asper
children.description: A flattened, rock-dwelling scorpion of African woodlands that shelters in crevices and under bark rather than digging burrows; the rough cuticle texture gives the species its name. Widespread across sub-Saharan forest zones.

children.name: Opisthacanthus elatus
children.description: A slender, crevice-dwelling scorpion found in Colombia and adjacent South America; the genus Opisthacanthus has a remarkable disjunct distribution spanning Africa and the Americas. Hides under bark and stones in humid tropical forest.

children.name: Opisthacanthus cayaporum
children.description: A South American Opisthacanthus described from Brazil; inhabits humid Atlantic forest and Cerrado woodland, concealing itself beneath bark and in narrow crevices. Part of the remarkable Africa-Americas disjunct distribution of the genus.

children.name: Opisthacanthus lepturus
children.description: A South American creeping scorpion with a notably slender metasoma compared to congeners; found in tropical forest habitats in the Guiana region. Flattened body form facilitates life in tight bark crevices.

children.name: Opisthacanthus madagascariensis
children.description: The sole Opisthacanthus known from Madagascar, inhabiting moist lowland and eastern rainforest; represents a westward island dispersal event from mainland Africa.

children.name: Opisthacanthus mccormicki
children.description: A West African Opisthacanthus described from forest zones of Nigeria and Cameroon; flattened body, hides under loose bark on large trees and fallen logs in humid forest.

children.name: Opisthacanthus rugiceps
children.description: An African creeping scorpion with a notably rugose (wrinkled) carapace surface; known from Central Africa's forest zone, sheltering under bark and in rock fissures.

children.name: Opisthacanthus validus
children.description: A stout-bodied West African Opisthacanthus from Nigeria and Cameroon; larger and more heavily built than many creeping scorpions, inhabiting forest floor debris and rocky crevices in humid lowland forest.

children.name: Opisthacanthus capensis
children.description: A southern African Opisthacanthus from the Cape and KwaZulu-Natal regions; flattened and bark-dwelling, preferring rocky fynbos and coastal forest habitats over the burrowing lifestyle of sympatric scorpions.

children.name: Opisthacanthus africanus
children.description: A widespread creeping scorpion of Central and East Africa, one of the more commonly encountered Opisthacanthus; lives beneath loose bark and in rock crevices in mixed woodland.

children.name: Cheloctonus jonesii
children.description: A southern African burrowing scorpion with a long, slender metasoma, inhabiting grasslands and digging shallow burrows in the Highveld and adjacent regions. The only species in its genus.

children.name: Hadogenes troglodytes
children.description: An extremely flattened southern African scorpion that lives in narrow rock cracks and is among the longest scorpions by body length; remarkably long-lived with extremely mild venom. The dorsoventral flattening is an adaptation to exploiting tight rock crevices.

children.name: Hadogenes paucidens
children.description: A flattened crevice-dwelling scorpion of southern African rocky outcrops, sheltering by day in tight cracks and foraging at night on cliff faces. Smaller than H. troglodytes.

children.name: Hadogenes bicolor
children.description: A slim, dorsoventrally compressed scorpion adapted to life within rock fissures of southern Africa, with striking contrasting body and limb coloration. Favors granite and sandstone outcrops.

children.name: Pandinoides cavimanus
children.description: An East African forest scorpion with a dark body and reddish pincers, formerly placed in Pandinus; common in the terrarium hobby and exported in numbers from East African range countries.
